coredata.dc:title: Degradation analysis and tanks-in-series modeling of lithium-ion batteries with state of health-adaptive charging strategies

coredata.dc:description: Dynamic operating conditions significantly impact the lifetime of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. While battery lifetime can be extended by optimizing charging profiles to reduce degradation, many existing charging optimization approaches are developed based on fixed, idealized full charge-discharge cycles. These differ from the random and partial charge-discharge behavior in real-world operation and do not consider the influence of battery degradation on charging current profile optimization. To address these limitations, this study designs two groups of battery aging tests to study charging optimization in real-world operation: one subjected to four fixed charging scenarios based on typical daily commuting patterns, and the other to dynamically changing charging scenarios based on state of health change. Capacity degradation, internal resistance increase, and charging time of all cells are analyzed and compared. Degradation modes such as loss of active materials and lithium inventory are examined through incremental capacity and differential voltage analyses. A novel tanks-in-series thermal-aging model is proposed to rapidly simulate battery behavior under dynamic charging, enabling rapid exploration of more charging scenarios constrained by experimental channels or costly to perform. Results demonstrate that dynamically switching charging strategies based on state of health can effectively extend battery lifetime while reducing overall charging time. Moreover, the model proves efficient in identifying optimal charging strategies. These findings offer valuable insights into charging optimization considering practical use scenarios, and present a promising tool for charging optimization.

Load profiles for aging tests: a) four real-world commuting scenarios, b) current profiles of four scenarios, c) fixed aging strategies (Group 1), d) dynamic aging strategies (Group 2), and the dots represent strategy switching points.

TIS-thermal-aging model and its verification. a) schematic of coupled model; b) schematic of interface of TIS model; c) OCV curves of positive and negative electrode; d) voltage, e) temperature, f) degradation of Cell A1; g) voltage, h) temperature, i) degradation of Cell D1. M = measurement, S = simulation results.

Comparison of SOH degradation and IR evolution for the two groups of tested cells. a) SOH degradation trajectories of cells in the first group; b) IR increase of cells in the first group; c) SOH degradation trajectories of cells in the second group; d) IR increase of cells in the second group. The squares indicate the scenario switching points.

Comparison of SOH, IR, and charging time among tested cells. a) and b) SOH degradation trajectories and IR comparison of Cells D1, D2 and C1; c) and d) SOH degradation trajectories and IR comparison of Cells C1, C2 and B1; e) and f) SOH degradation trajectories and IR comparison of Cells B1, B2 and A1; g) the charging time of different cell pairs until SOH intersection point; h) the capacity gain and the reduction of charging time.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dynamic operating conditions significantly impact the lifetime of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. While battery lifetime can be extended by optimizing charging profiles to reduce degradation, many existing charging optimization approaches are developed based on fixed, idealized full charge-discharge cycles. These differ from the random and partial charge-discharge behavior in real-world operation and do not consider the influence of battery degradation on charging current profile optimization. To address these limitations, this study designs two groups of battery aging tests to study charging optimization in real-world operation: one subjected to four fixed charging scenarios based on typical daily commuting patterns, and the other to dynamically changing charging scenarios based on state of health change. Capacity degradation, internal resistance increase, and charging time of all cells are analyzed and compared. Degradation modes such as loss of active materials and lithium inventory are examined through incremental capacity and differential voltage analyses. A novel tanks-in-series thermal-aging model is proposed to rapidly simulate battery behavior under dynamic charging, enabling rapid exploration of more charging scenarios constrained by experimental channels or costly to perform. Results demonstrate that dynamically switching charging strategies based on state of health can effectively extend battery lifetime while reducing overall charging time. Moreover, the model proves efficient in identifying optimal charging strategies. These findings offer valuable insights into charging optimization considering practical use scenarios, and present a promising tool for charging optimization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The lifetime and capacity degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles (EVs) are strongly influenced by their dynamic operation conditions [1,2]. These conditions are characterized by factors such as temperature [3], state-of-charge (SOC) [4], current rate [5], charging duration and rest period [6], and depth of discharge (DOD) [7]. These factors are intricately linked to chemical and physical side reactions within the battery. For instance, high current rates can lead to particle cracking [8]. High temperatures accelerate the growth of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film [9]. Low temperatures promote lithium plating, and high SOC results in cathode instability [10]. The side reactions ultimately lead to the loss of active materials (LAM), loss of lithium-ion inventory (LLI), and increased internal resistance (IR) [11,12]. Battery lifetime can be extended by maintaining operating conditions that minimize side reactions and by regulating charging strategies.

In practical applications, thermal management systems are designed to maintain the battery temperature around room temperature and prevent unsafe or extreme temperature conditions, thereby limiting degradation [13]. Charging strategies optimization can be categorized as model-free and model-based methods. Model-free methods, such as boost charging [14], multistage constant current charging (MSCC) [15], and pulse charging [16], aim to maximize charging speed while minimizing degradation by adjusting current profiles. Model-based methods combine physics-based and aging models to capture battery internal dynamics, employing optimization tools to derive health-conscious charging strategies. Aging models incorporate both cell-level and electrode-level aging information [17]. However, most strategies are derived for fresh cells and remain fixed throughout the battery's lifetime without considering the variation of battery state-of-health (SOH) [18,19]. Only a few studies incorporate SOH into charging strategy optimization. For example, one strategy minimizes capacity loss from side reactions at different aging stages respectively using coupled models [20]. Another adjusts the constant current (CC) cut-off voltage in constant current constant voltage (CCCV) charging at different SOHs, gradually increasing the cut-off voltage in subsequent cycles until the end-of-life (EOL) or reaching maximum voltage, potentially extending the cycle life by 50 % [21]. Nevertheless, most charging optimization studies remain at the laboratory scale and do not explicitly consider vehicle-level implementation [22]. In addition, charging optimization often neglects information from real-world scenarios such as daily commuting patterns [23]. Furthermore, capacity degradation is influenced by both charging and discharging profiles. However, most studies simplify discharging to CC-discharging, which does not adequately represent the complex and dynamic load profiles encountered in real-world EV operation [1,24].

Physics-based modeling can simulate the battery behavior, quantify the degradation modes and be used for further optimization control [25]. Main physics-based modeling technologies include electrochemical models, aging models, equivalent circuit models [26], and thermal models [27,28]. The pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model is the foundational and most classic electrochemical model, describing the intercalation and deintercalation process using partial differential equations (PDEs) [18]. In the P2D model, Fick's second law governs the solid-phase diffusion, and Butler-Volmer kinetics is used to couple the transport of lithium ions between solid-phase active materials and electrolyte [29]. To improve the simulation accuracy for batteries at high C-rates or other conditions, more advanced modeling techniques are proposed based on specific limit factors. For instance, Xu et al. modeled the anode with Fick's law and phase separation model, and modeled the cathode using a 2D reconstruction structure to capture charge transfer resistance for fast charging [30]. However, the complexities of parameter identification and the slow-solving process of the P2D model and its advanced variant are not efficient for real-time simulation and optimization of charging strategies [31]. As a result, the single particle model (SPM) was proposed and widely adopted as it significantly simplifies the governing equations by describing the electrode as a single spherical particle and focusing solely on ion transport between the electrodes [32]. To account for non-uniform reaction distributions within the particles, concentration distributions are typically approximated using polynomial profiles of varying terms [33]. The main limitation of SPM, however, is that it neglects concentration and charge variations in the electrolyte, increasing the estimation error of the model. To address this issue, the SPM with electrolyte (SPMe) was developed, incorporating electrolyte diffusion and the corresponding boundary conditions into the SPM framework. Three methods are used to solve electrolyte concentration distribution: discretizing the electrolyte's PDE using finite difference methods [34], approximating concentration distribution with polynomial equations [35], and using transfer functions to describe the relationship between electrolyte concentration and time [36]. Nevertheless, solving PDEs remains inefficient, and the assumptions underlying polynomial methods are not always robust.

An efficient approach to reducing model complexity is to simplify the model based on physical principles, while ensuring that models involve differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) rather than PDEs. For this reason, our group proposed the Tanks-in-series (TIS) model. The key idea of the TIS model is to use the volume-averaging and gradient interpolation concepts to simplify the full p2D while achieving a similar accuracy. This approach allows for the description of the electrolyte concentration and charge conservation using only DAEs, eliminating the need for PDEs and polynomial profile approximation [37]. However, the TIS model has not been coupled with a thermal model and aging model for modeling of dynamic operation of the battery till now [38].

To fill the above research gaps and investigate the possibility of extending battery lifetime by changing dynamic charging/discharging based on different SOHs, we conducted three-year aging tests and proposed an efficient model. The overall flowchart of our work is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, nickel-manganese-cobalt-oxide (NMC) batteries were aged at four different charging scenarios in daily commuter scenarios and four varied scenarios based on different SOHs. Then, the post-processing methods including incremental capacity analysis (ICA) [39] and differential voltage analysis (DVA) [40] were used to quantify the degradation by LLI and LAM. The TIS coupled with thermal and aging models was proposed to analyze the degradation of batteries under dynamic operations with switching operation conditions and to research additional charging strategies. The coupled model was validated by measured parameters such as voltage, temperature, and capacity before its application. The capacity loss caused by SEI growth and lithium plating was also analyzed.

To illustrate this work, the article is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the designed aging tests and relevant analysis methods. Section 3 introduces the proposed TIS coupled model and preliminary verification results. Section 4 presents the experiment and simulation results. Section 5 is the discussion and limitations. Section 6 summarizes the article. The key contributions of this study are as follows:
(1)
A novel aging test is designed and implemented, in which charging scenarios are adjusted based on the battery SOH, and the resulting degradation behavior is systematically investigated.
(2)
A TIS–thermal–aging model is developed to capture battery behavior under dynamic charging switching scenarios. By formulating all governing equations in an ODE-based framework by physical assumption, the model reduces computational cost while retaining accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tested batteries and designed aging tests
ce:section.ce:para: Eight commercial Samsung SDI INR21700-40T cells, each with a nominal capacity of 4 Ah, were utilized in aging tests to investigate the degradation behavior under dynamic charging and discharging conditions, adjusted based on different SOH levels. The detailed battery parameters, obtained from the manufacturer's datasheet and used for modeling, are summarized in Table S1.

Eight cells were divided into two groups for cycling aging tests, all placed in a Memmert thermal chamber at 35
°C
. This elevated temperature was chosen to accelerate battery aging. In the first group, each of the four cells was aged under a different, fixed load profile. These load profiles were designed to replicate real-world commuting scenarios, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Cycle (WLTC) was employed to represent the operation or dynamic discharging conditions (i.e., 1 WLTC
≈
11.7 % DOD), while charging was performed using a CCCV protocol. In Scenario 1, the battery was charged after each commuting event, alternating between one WLTC and one CCCV charging, repeated twice. In Scenario 2, the battery was charged once with 0.5C CCCV after two consecutive commute uses (i.e., 2WLTCs). In Scenario 3, the battery was charged once with 0.5C CCCV after long-term use (i.e., 6WLTCs), which was slow charging [41]. In Scenario 4, unlike Scenario 3, the battery was fast-charged at 1.5C after six commuting events. The current profiles of the four scenarios are depicted in Fig. 2b. In the second group, the other four cells were aged under dynamically adjusted charging scenarios based on their SOHs. The WLTC was also applied to represent dynamic discharging. The EOL of the batteries is assumed to correspond to a 20 % SOH degradation. Within this SOH window, an appropriate switching threshold is required to evaluate the effect of adjusting charging strategies. If the threshold is set too large, the benefits of SOH-based charging adjustments may not be clearly observable. Conversely, a very small threshold would require additional test channels to implement the fixed aging scenarios. In this study, four charging scenarios were designed, and dividing the 20 % SOH window by four results in a 5 % threshold. Therefore, a 5 % change in SOH was selected as the switching criterion. When the SOH loss exceeded 5 % each time, the load profile was adjusted accordingly. Specifically, Cell A2 switched its charging strategy from Scenario 1 to Scenario 2, then to Scenario 3, and finally to Scenario 4 after each 5 % decrease in SOH. Cell B2 switched from Scenario 2 to Scenario 1 after a 5 % change in SOH. Cell C2 switched from Scenario 3 to Scenario 2, and then to Scenario 1 after each 5 % change in SOH. Cell D2 switched from Scenario 4 to Scenario 3, then to Scenario 2, and finally to Scenario 1 after each 5 % change in SOH, following the reverse order of Cell A2. During the experiments, to verify the previously observed conclusions, the switching point of cell D2 was exceptionally set at a 10 % SOH degradation threshold. The fixed aging strategies and dynamic adjustment strategies are presented in Fig. 2c and d. The aging profiles were conducted using a NEWARE battery cycler, and the test setup is shown in Fig. S1.

To measure capacity and IR and to ensure a fair comparison of battery lifetime among all cells, reference performance tests (RPTs) were conducted simultaneously at 25
°C
after every 100 FECs, maintaining the same throughput and storage duration across all cells. In each round of aging, the four aforementioned load profiles were repeated 428, 428, 143, and 143 times, respectively. These repetition numbers are calculated based on the ratio of the initial ampere-hour throughput to the nominal capacity and are designed to correspond to 100 FECs. The FECs used in the subsequent comparisons are based on the actual accumulated FECs during cycling, rather than being a simple multiple of the cycle number. This simultaneous testing ensured consistent idling time across all batteries. Battery capacity was measured during 0.5C discharging and 0.5C CC-CV charging. A relaxation time of 1 h was considered before charging and discharging. The discharge capacity was taken as the measured capacity. The IR was measured using a hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC) test at SOCs of 100 %, 90 %, 70 %, 50 %, 30 %, 20 %, and 0 %. Pulse amplitudes were set at 0.5C and 1.5C for each discharging and charging direction, with each pulse lasting 20 s. The relaxation time was 15 min.

To quantitatively analyze the degradation modes, post-processing methods including ICA and DVA were applied. Incremental capacity (IC) curves, derived as the differential of charge with respect to the voltage interval (dU), require low current measurements to approximate equilibrium conditions [42]. Conversely, differential voltage (DV) is the inverse of the IC. The calculations of IC and DV follow [43]:

LLI is identified by reduced peak heights with a shift toward lower or higher voltages (horizontal axis) in IC curves, along with shifts toward lower capacities (horizontal axis) in DV curves, due to decreased availability of Li-ions for intercalation/deintercalation [44]. LAM manifests as reduced IC peak heights at nearly constant voltages and reduced depth of the DV valleys at nearly constant capacity, indicating diminished active material [45,46]. Considering the clarity of the curve variations, IC peak height reduction is used to quantify LAM, while DV curve shifts toward lower capacities indicate LLI. Given the constraints of experimental channels and the time-intensive nature of testing, modeling is essential to explore a wider range of conditions, verify experimental trends, and enable predictions. A coupled TIS-thermal-aging model was developed to further describe the battery response in the above tests and identify the charging scenarios that achieve the longest lifetime with shorter charging time. Different from data-driven methods, the accurate physics-based model can derive the battery's multi-physics internal and output response including voltage, temperature, and capacity degradation directly from any input current, without the need for extensive training data and time-consuming aging tests [47,48]. This work is the first to apply the accurate and efficient TIS model in combination with thermal and aging models to simulate dynamic, SOH-based charging adjustment strategies after literature analysis [38]. The diagram of the coupled model is illustrated in Fig. 3 a.

TIS model simplifies the P2D model by treating electrolyte domains of electrodes and separator as separate tanks and applying the volume-averaging method in each tank to reduce the PDEs governing mass transport and charge transport into ordinary differential equations (ODEs). Interpolation methods are employed to handle the interface boundary conditions between neighboring tanks. As shown in Fig. 3b, the interfacial flux is calculated using interpolation by dividing the concentration or potential difference (i.e., the driving force) by the characteristic length.

Specifically, for mass transport within solid particles of each electrode tank, the average and surface stoichiometry (i.e., the ratio of the lithium concentration to the maximum lithium concentration

Detailed derivation of TIS model is referred to Ref. [37], which shows the matchable simulation accuracy with P2D models. Before further application, the model was first validated. For simplicity, the simulated and measured voltages of Cell A1 and Cell D1 are presented in Fig. 3d and g, with RMSE values of 16.5 mV and 65.5 mV, respectively. Voltage validation results for the other cells are shown in Fig. S3, where the maximum RMSE is 88.5 mV for Cell C1 due to the rapid fluctuations of WLTC. These results demonstrate the good simulation performance of the TIS model.

The simulated and measured temperatures of Cells A1 and D1 are presented in Fig. 3e and h, with RMSE values of 0.85 K and 1.96 K, respectively. The results show that the simulated temperatures closely track the measured temperatures during discharge, accurately reflecting the temperature trends. However, during charging, the simulated temperatures are lower than the measured values, primarily because the measurements reflect the higher surface temperatures rather than the volume-averaged temperatures simulated by the model. Thermal verification results for the other cells are provided in Fig. S3, with a maximum RMSE of 0.9K.

The degradation verification for the remaining first-group and second-group cells is shown in Fig. S3, with a maximum error of 1.36 %.

Model validation results for voltage, temperature, and degradation across all tested cells are further summarized in Table 1 . Based on the above introduction and verification, the proposed coupled model accurately simulates dynamic charging and discharging protocols with high precision and simulation speed. It can also be extended to explore additional or incomplete test results and other potential scenarios. The model was developed in MATLAB, using ode15s as the ODE solver.

To analyze the degradation behavior of two groups of cells, we compared their SOH degradation trajectories and the increase in IR. Fig. 4 a and b present the SOH degradation trajectory and the normalized IR increase (measured at 0.5C and 50 % of SOC) for the first group of cells aged under fixed load profiles. Here the normalized IR was to minimize the impact of cell-to-cell variability. The degradation severity in this group follows the order: D1 > C1 > B1 >A1. A higher charging current and deeper DoD result in accelerated degradation. Specifically, Cell D1 exhibits slightly faster degradation than Cell C1, primarily due to the higher charging current of Scenario 4 compared to Scenario 3. Cell C1 degrades significantly more than Cell B1, mainly owing to the deeper DoD of Scenario 3 relative to Scenario 2. The degradation of Cell B1 is slightly greater than that of Cell A1, as Scenario 1 has a shallower DoD before recharging than Scenario 2. Fig. 4c and d show the SOH and IR variation for the second group of cells. The square markers indicate the switching points between different scenarios, where notable changes in the degradation slopes are observed. Table S2 further summarizes the SOH degradation slopes between each switching point. The results indicate that the degradation slopes depend solely on the specific scenarios, rather than the stage of degradation. As the FEC increases, the degradation curves of Cells D2 and A2 tend to intersect, which can be attributed to their opposing aging strategies.

To further evaluate the cumulative benefits of dynamically adjusting charging scenarios based on the battery SOHs, we compare the SOH degradation and IR increase among relevant cells from two groups. The selected cells from Group 2 experienced more fast-charging scenarios before switching to the same scenarios as the corresponding cells from Group 1. Fig. 5 a and b illustrate the SOH degradation and IR increase of Cell D2, D1, and C1. As shown in Fig. 5a, Cell D2 exhibits a slower SOH degradation than Cell D1 due to switching to the switch to less aggressive aging scenarios. Notably, after switching Cell D2's aging scenario from Scenario 4 to Scenario 3, the total SOH degradation of Cell D2 between 365 and 1067 FEC is lower than that of Cell C1. The cumulative charging time for Cell D2 and Cell C1 until their SOH intersection point is 2253.4 h and 2556.6 h, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5g. This suggests that cumulative degradation can be reduced and the total charging time shortened by switching to slower charging scenarios. In Fig. 5b, the IR of Cell D2 is lower than that of Cell D1 and Cell C2. Although the scenario switch plays a role, cell-to-cell variability in IR may also be a contributing factor. The slope of Cell D2 tends to decrease after switching to less aggressive scenarios, whereas Cell C1's internal resistance continues to increase steadily with a fixed slope. Similarly, Fig. 5c compares Cell C1, C2 and B1. After Cell C2 transitions from Scenario 3 to Scenarios 2 and 1 sequentially, its SOH becomes higher than that of Cell B1 beyond 1000 FEC. The charging time of Cell C2 and Cell B1 until their SOH intersection is 2754 h and 3624 h, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5g. The IR of Cell C2 is lower than that of Cell C1, and its rate of increase gradually decreases compared to the near constant slope of Cell B1, as shown in Fig. 5d. Fig. 5e compares Cell B1, B2, and A1, showing that the SOH of Cell B2 surpasses that of Cell A1 beyond 840 FEC after transitioning from Scenario 2 to Scenario 1. Their respective charging times up to the intersection point are 2574.1 h (Cell B2) and 2707.3 h (Cell A1). Due to the negligible IR difference between Cell A1 and B1, the slope of Cell B2 appears to remain unchanged. These findings indicate that adjusting charging strategies based on SOH allows for reduced cumulative degradation while maintaining short total charging time.

Fig. 5h summarizes the capacity gain and reduction in charging time around the SOH intersection points (same FEC). The capacity gains for the three compared cells are 1.3 %, 0.39 %, and 0.65 %, while the corresponding charging time reductions are 11.86 %, 9.31 %, and 4.92 %. Fig. S5 extends this analysis to the entire FEC span, showing overall capacity gains of −1.05 %, 0.76 %, and 2.25 % for the pairs Cell D2 vs. Cell C1, Cell C2 vs. Cell B1, and Cell B2 and Cell A1, respectively. The corresponding reductions in charging time are 0.3 %, 4.47 %, and 6.17 %.

IC and DV curves are employed to quantify the degradation modes. Fig. 6 a–6d illustrate the IC curves of Cell A1, A2, D1, and D2, while IC and DV of the remaining cells are shown in Fig. S6. As the batteries degrade, the IC peaks decrease in height and shift toward higher voltages. Compared to Cell A1, the peak spacing in the IC curves of Cell A2 increases significantly with aging. Conversely, the peak pacing of Cell D2 narrows over time compared to Cell D1. Fig. 6e compares the LAM of Cell D1, D2, C1, and A2. Following the switch from Scenario 4 to Scenario 3, the LAM of Cell D2 is slightly lower than that of Cell D1 and marginally higher than that of Cell C1. The slope of Cell A2's LAM increases after each scenario switch, with its final LAM expected to intersect that of Cell D2. Fig. 6f illustrates the comparison among Cell C1, Cell C2 and Cell B1. Prior to switching from Scenario 3 to Scenario 2, the LAM of Cell C2 overlaps with that of Cell C1. After the switch, although the LAM growth rate of Cell C2 decreases, it remains higher than that of Cell B1 due to its substantial accumulated LAM. A similar trend is observed for Cell B2 compared to Cell A1, as shown in Fig. 6g.

Fig. 6h–k presents the DV curves of Cell A1, A2, D1, and D2. As the batteries age, the DV curves shift to lower capacities, and the depth of valleys decreases. Additionally, the maximum charge decreases with degradation. Fig. 6l compares the evolution of LLI among Cells D1, D2, C1 and A2. Cell D1 shows slightly higher LLI values than Cell C1. For Cell D2, its LLI nearly overlaps with that of Cell D1 prior to the switch from Scenario 4 to Scenario 3. After the switch, its LLI becomes comparable to that of Cell C1 with a reduced slope. In the case of Cell A2, the LLI slope gradually increases over time, and its final LLI value approaches that of Cell D2. Fig. 6m shows the LLI behavior of Cells C1, C2 and B1. Cell C2 initially shows a similar LLI to Cell C1 before switching from Scenario 3 to Scenario 2. After switching, its LLI is close to Cell B1 with a lower slope. Similarly, Cell B2's LLI growth becomes comparable to that of Cell A1 after switching from Scenario 2 to Scenario 1, as shown in Fig. 6n. Notably, the increase of LLI differs from that of LAM; LLI tends to partially return toward baseline scenarios after switching to less aggressive charging scenarios, whereas LAM does not exhibit such recovery. This difference can be attributed to the intrinsic reversibility of the underlying mechanisms: LLI is influenced by lithium availability and SEI dynamics, which can partially recover under milder stress conditions and controlled SOC windows, whereas LAM reflects irreversible physical degradation of electrode materials, such as particle fracture or active surface loss, which cannot be restored. In short, higher charging current and deeper DoD are associated with larger increases in LLI and LAM.

Given that not all cells have yet reached their EOL and that many potentially beneficial charging strategies remain unexplored, we propose a highly accurate and computationally efficient TIS-coupled model to simulate battery behavior under dynamic operation conditions. Fig. 7 a presents the simulated lifetime of all cells based on a 20 % capacity degradation EOL criterion. In this simulation, the designed charging scenarios are sequentially applied and switched every 5 % SOH degradation until the EOL threshold is reached. Cell A1 demonstrates the longest lifetime with 2452 FECs, followed by Cell B2 with 2288 FECs and less charging time. Cell B1 and Cell C2 both reach 1842 FECs, while Cell D2 outperforms Cell C1 than 395 FECs, achieving 1377 FECs versus 982 FECs, respectively. Cell D1 shows the shortest lifetime with 961 FECs. Notably, although Cell A2 and Cell D2 undergo aging under the same four charging scenarios but in reversed order, Cell A2 still exhibits a longer lifetime of 1629 FECs. One possible explanation is that degradation occurring at an early stage is often irreversible. Fig. 7b illustrates the average stoichiometry of the positive electrode in Cell D1. The model can capture its variation in one round and all aging rounds. Since the primary objective of this work is to investigate degradation behavior, other internal variables are not analyzed in this study.

Fig. 7c–e further compare the SOH loss trajectories. As shown in Fig. 7c, the SOH degradation trajectory of Cell D1 nearly overlaps with that of Cell C1. After switching scenarios, Cell D2 exhibits a longer lifetime than Cell D1; however, the faster degradation of Cell D2 in the initial stage leads to 252 fewer FECs accumulated compared to Cell A2, indicating that the subsequent slower aging phase does not fully compensate for the early faster degradation. In Fig. 7d, Cell C2 demonstrates a longer lifetime than Cell C1 and gradually approaches the degradation trajectory of Cell B1 following the scenario switch. Similarly, the degradation trajectory of Cell B2 converges towards that of Cell A1 as shown in Fig. 7e.

Fig. 7f–h compare the capacity loss attributed to SEI growth. The variation in capacity loss for all cells due to SEI growth follows an opposite trend to that of SOH degradation. Therefore, a detailed comparative analysis is omitted. Fig. S7 presents the capacity loss resulting from lithium plating, showing that the capacity loss caused by SEI growth is significantly greater than that caused by lithium plating.

We designed and conducted a three-year battery aging test with dynamically changing charging scenarios adapted to the SOH, unlike most existing tests that use fixed full charging/discharging profiles. The results demonstrate that charging time can be reduced while also mitigating capacity degradation, with a maximum charging time reduction of up to 10 %. This finding provides actionable insights for users or manufacturers to achieve shorter charging times and longer battery lifetimes in daily charging. Nevertheless, due to experimental constraints, we analyzed only four switching strategies based on a 5 % SOH degradation threshold. In future work, more switching strategies will be explored to search the optimal strategies that maximize capacity retention and minimize charging time.

To simulate the dynamic behavior of aging tests and enable early prediction of degradation trends, we proposed a TIS-thermal-aging model. Compared with the conventional P2D model, which requires spatial discretization in both the electrode thickness and particle radius directions, the TIS model demands less computational time and resources. By employing a volume-lumped method with interpolation in the electrolyte domain and a polynomial approximation of solid-phase concentration, the TIS model avoids spatial discretization and converts the governing PDEs into a system of ODEs based on physical assumptions. For a single-cycle CC charge and discharge simulation, the MATLAB-based TIS model requires approximately 1.4 s, whereas a COMSOL-based P2D model requires about 20 s to achieve comparable accuracy. Compared with SPM/SPMe models, the TIS model achieves higher accuracy by explicitly resolving electrolyte-related variables based on physical assumptions. Our previous work demonstrated that the TIS model achieved an accuracy of 20 mV with a computation time of 2 ms under a 5C discharge, compared with the SPM, which showed an error of 60 mV and a computation time of 1000 ms when solved using the Finite Difference method [37]. Simulations in this work were performed on the Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-10210U CPU @ 1.60 GHz 2.11 GHz processor, and the total simulation times until EOL were counted as follows: 76.88 h, 61.80 h, 42.77 h, and 51.5 h for Cell A1, B1, C1, and D1, respectively; and 49.68 h, 66.97 h, 62.16 h, and 49.27 h for Cell A2, B2, C2, and D2, respectively. All simulation durations are significantly shorter than the 3-year experimental period. The proposed model will be used for future search of optimal charging switching strategies and optimization of single-cycle charging profile.

For real-time deployment of the proposed model and charging strategies in a battery management system (BMS), further comparison of different switching strategies is necessary to identify the optimal approach. The TIS-thermal-aging model is fully ODE-based, enabling low computational load suitable for MCU-level implementation. In practical applications, SOH can be estimated using physics-based methods, including the proposed approach, or machine learning techniques. Within an allowable SOH estimation error range (smaller than the switching threshold), the BMS can inform users about the current charging strategy and regulate the charging current accordingly. The approach requires only standard BMS sensors (voltage, current, temperature) and does not need additional complex instrumentation [49].

Prior to the aging tests and subsequent normalization, grading and pre-tests were conducted to minimize cell-to-cell variability. Fig. 8 shows the box plots of the pre-test capacity results for all tested cells. The dotted line represents the mean discharge capacity of all cells measured in the final pre-test cycle (i.e., the fifth cycle in this work). As shown, the pre-test capacities of all cells are close to the mean value, with a maximum relative deviation of 0.74 % from the nominal capacity at the fifth cycle. These pre-test capacity values can therefore serve as a baseline for SOH normalization and subsequent aging analysis. Nevertheless, the sample size is still limited, and future work will involve testing more cells under each condition to further reduce cell-to-cell variability [50,51].

The normal operating temperature of EVs usually does not exceed 35
°C
due to the thermal management system. In this work, we set 35
°C
as the aging temperature to accelerate battery degradation, which may induce additional side reactions. Future tests should be conducted at temperatures closer to room temperature. In addition, this work mainly focuses on cell-level LLI and LAM analysis. Future work will concentrate on electrode-level degradation analysis and application [52].

To investigate whether dynamically adjusting charging scenarios based on SOH can extend battery lifetime while reducing charging time, we designed four representative daily commuting charging scenarios and applied them sequentially in an additional aging test group. A novel TIS-thermal-aging model was also proposed to simulate the dynamic behavior of the tested cells and enable exploration of optimal switching strategies. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1)
Higher charging currents and deeper DoD accelerate battery degradation. However, switching from fast to slower charging scenarios can extend battery lifetime while reducing overall charging time. After switching to more aggressive scenarios, the slopes of LLI and LAM increased accordingly. LLI tended to return toward baseline values after switching, whereas LAM did not.
(2)
The proposed TIS-thermal-aging model demonstrated high accuracy and fast computation. Simulations showed that switching charging scenarios can increase the number of FECs. Even when cells experienced the same scenarios in reverse order, cells starting with slower charging exhibited longer lifetimes than those starting with faster charging.

These findings provide practical insights for users and manufacturers in optimizing charging strategies to simultaneously reduce degradation and shorten charging time in daily commuting applications. BMS can incorporate fast, physics-based models for more accurate control. Future work will explore additional charging scenarios with larger sample sizes, multi-scale analyses, and profiles that closely match real-world operating conditions, including temperature effects.
